segments.s047-g145: از آن تاکنون is an adverbial phrase 'from that until now' placed at the clause start. کین او is a genitival phrase 'his vengeance' with the possessor اُ following the noun (an older Persian order). کس نخواست literally 'no one wanted/sought' uses کس ('no one') as the subject and نخواست (past of خواستن) meaning 'did not want/seek' (the negation is carried by the sense/context 'no one'). The object (کین او) appears before the subject and verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s047-g146: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause linked to the preceding statement ('whom/that'). پشت زمانه is a nominative/genitive phrase 'the backing/support of Time/Fortune' (پشت = support/protection, زمانه = fortune/the world). ندیدیم is first-person plural past negative 'we did not see' (ن- prefix + دیدیم), and راست ('true/firm') is a predicate adjective placed after the verb; the whole clause means 'whom we did not see to be truly backed by Fortune.'
segments.s047-g147: نه is a negative/exclamatory particle negating the clause that follows. خوب آمدی is the verb آمدی (2nd-person past ‘you came’) with خوب as an adverb/adjective ('well' / 'in good fashion'), so the phrase means 'you did not come well' or 'it was not good that you came.' با + دو + فرزند is the preposition with the numeral and noun 'with two children'; خویش + ش is the possessive construction (خویش 'one's own/kin' with enclitic ش 'his/her/its'), here giving 'his own children' (in context often rendered 'your two sons' or 'his two sons' depending on the addressee).
segments.s047-g148: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. جنگ را uses را as the definite/object marker to highlight 'the battle/war' as the direct object/topic. کردمی is an archaic past-tense form of کردن (here a narrative/past form) — historically used as a past tense; grammatically it marks the action 'made/did' (the available lexeme sense here is first-person past in the lexicon, though in classical usage forms can vary). دست پیش is a small nominal phrase: دست 'hand' and پیش 'before/in advance' combine idiomatically to mean 'the first move' or 'initiative' (literally 'the hand before'), so the clause asks where the fighting was begun by the first move.
segments.s047-g149: کنون is the adverb 'now.' زان is a contracted prepositional/deictic 'from that' (از + آن), introducing origin. درختی is the noun 'tree' with the indefinite/qualifying suffix -ی. که is the relative pronoun 'which/that' linking the relative clause. دشمن is the noun 'enemy/foe.' بکند is a classical-present/optative form of کردن (the b- prefix is the older present/optative marker); in epic narrative such a form can render an action the enemy 'does' or 'has done' (here best understood as 'which the enemy struck/acted upon').
segments.s047-g150: برومند is an adjective/epithet meaning 'vigorous/lofty/noble.' شاخی is a noun derived from شاخ (branch) with the adjectival/relational -ی, here 'a branch' (poetically placed after the epithet). برآمد is the past form 'arose/issued forth' (3rd-person singular). بلند is an adjective 'tall/high' placed after the verb for poetic emphasis, qualifying the branch as having risen high.
segments.s047-g151: بیاید is an optative/jussive or narrative-present form of آمدن — here 'may (he) come' or 'he comes' (a wish or dramatic present). کنون 'now' follows the verb for immediacy. چون is the comparative 'like/as.' هژبر is used as a proper name/epithet (here treated as a personal name), and ژیان follows as part of the epithet (literally 'life' but functioning as part of the name/title), so the phrase means 'like H(e)jbar Zhiyān' or 'as the mighty Hejbar of life.'
segments.s047-g152: به introduces the circumstance/means and here reads 'with' / 'by' (rendered 'with' or 'consumed by' in idiomatic English). کین is the noun 'vengeance/hatred.' پدر is 'father.' تنگ بسته is a compound participial expression: تنگ 'tight/narrow' plus بسته 'bound/tied/closed' — together meaning 'tightly bound/constricted.' میان literally means 'middle' or 'in the midst' and in this collocation refers to the breast/heart area ('in his breast/middle'), so the whole phrase describes someone whose chest/heart is tightly bound by his father's vengeance (i.e., driven/strangled by filial grudge).
segments.s047-g65: بپوییم is a first-person-plural hortative/subjunctive ("let us…") formed on the verb پویدن (to go/seek) and here means "let us go/seek." تا introduces a purpose/result clause ("so that/in order that"). آب و رنجش دهیم is the clause expressing what will be given: آب and رنجش are coordinated direct objects of دهیم, and دهیم is a present-subjunctive/optative first-person-plural ("we may/we will give"). Together the clause reads literally “let us go so that we give water and (give) suffering/hardship.”
segments.s047-g66: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning "when." تازه شود is a short subordinate clause with شود (third-person singular of شدن, "become") meaning "when it becomes fresh/renewed" (the subject is implied, e.g. the crown). تاج و گنجش دهیم is the main clause that follows the temporal clause: تاج and گنجش are coordinated objects of دهیم ("we will give the crown and (his) treasure"); note that the enclitic -ش on گنجش can attach to the second coordinated noun and be understood as applying to the first as well in classical Persian coordination. دهیم remains the hortative/optative first-person-plural "we give/will give."
segments.s047-g67: فرستاده آمد is a simple past clause 'a messenger came' (subject + past verb). دلی پر سخن follows as a stative/adverbial description: literally 'a heart full of speech' (دلی 'heart' + پر 'full (of)' + سخن 'words/speech'), describing the messenger's inner state; Persian here omits linking particles, so the noun phrase apposes or complements the coming.
segments.s047-g68: سخن را uses را as a topical/definite marker (marks 'the speech' as the theme). نه ... نه is a correlative negative 'neither ... nor'; the predicate is 'سر بود پیدا' and 'بن' parallels 'سر', so the line says 'the speech had neither a head/beginning nor a root/base seen.' بود is the past copula and پیدا is predicative 'visible/apparent'.
segments.s047-g69: This is a list introduced by (poetic) با-forms: 'ابا پیل و با گنج و با خواسته' = 'with an elephant and with treasure and with a request.' The initial ابا is a poetic/orthographic variant of با meaning 'with' (here sense not in the provided list), each با governs a following noun; خواسته functions as 'request/wish.'
segments.s047-g70: به درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the king's court' (به + noun), آمد is the past 'he came/arrived', and آراسته is a past participle/adjective 'adorned' functioning as a predicative complement ('came adorned' or 'arrived in finery'). Word order puts the locative phrase before the verb, which is common in Persian narrative.
segments.s047-g71: آگهی رسید is a V-S order meaning 'the news reached' or 'news arrived.' به شاه آفریدون is the dative phrase 'to King Afrīdūn' (به marking the indirect object/recipient). Persian allows the verb before its subject or object in poetic diction, so 'آگهی' (the news) appears after رسید.
segments.s047-g72: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb 'he commanded/ordered/said.' تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that/so that' (here introducing the content of the order). تخت شاهنشهی is the object phrase 'the imperial throne' (تخت 'throne' + شاهنشهی 'imperial/royal'), the clause is elliptic (the verb of making/setting up is implied).
segments.s047-g73: به introduces the clothing-material phrase and here functions as a prepositional marker meaning 'in/with' (classical usage with garments). دیبای چینی is a noun phrase 'Chinese brocade' (دیبا 'brocade/silk' modified by چینی 'Chinese'). بیاراستند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb (آراستن with the prefix بی-), 'they adorned/dressed (him)', agreeing with an understood plural agent.
segments.s047-g74: کلاه کیانی is a noun + attributive name/adjective: 'the Kayanian (Kiani) hat/crown' (اسمِ خاص or adjectival use). بپیراستند is again 3rd-person plural past of پیراستن/آراستن with prosthetic ب-, 'they adorned' or 'they put (it) on', and it continues the same subject/action as the previous verb (coordination of two past actions).
segments.s047-g75: نشست is the past/preterite verb 'sat' (here third-person singular). از بر تخت is an older/preclassical locative combination ('از بر' functioning like 'on/upon' in epic style) and تخت پیروزه is a compound noun 'turquoise throne' (پیروزه an adjective/noun indicating turquoise or turquoise-colored), while شاه is the possessor 'of the king'. Word order is verb → locative phrase, typical of older Persian epic diction.
segments.s047-g76: چو is the simile marker 'like/as'. سرو سهی is a descriptive simile 'a dark/tall cypress' (سرو 'cypress' modified by سهی 'dark/black' or a poetic epithet). بر سرش puts the following image in relation to 'his head' (سر + possessive ش), and گرد ماه here indicates 'the moon's roundness/circle about (him)' with گرد used in the sense 'around/about' (poetic word order places the modifying phrase after the simile).
segments.s047-g77: This is a series of prepositional phrases introduced by (apparent) ابا/با 'with' listing the regalia: تاج 'crown', طوق 'necklace/collar', باگوشوار 'with earrings'. The initial 'ابا' appears as a variant/phonetic form of با (with); if read as the refusal verb it would not fit the context, so here it functions as the preposition 'with' in the epic register, coordinating items with و 'and'.
segments.s047-g78: چنان چون is a reinforcing pair meaning 'thus, just as' or 'so that' (چنان = 'such/so', چون = 'as/like'). بود is the past copula 'was'. در خور شهریار is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'worthy/suitable for the king' (در + خور + شهریار); خور here is archaic/poetic and denotes 'worthiness' or suitability in this expression (see note). The whole clause states that, with those adornments, he was appropriate for a sovereign.
segments.s047-g79: خجسته is an epithet 'auspicious/blessed' modifying the name منوچهر (Manuchehr). بر دست شاه is a locative phrase 'on the king's hand' or 'at the king's hand' (بر + دست + possessive شاه). The sequence places Manuchehr in physical relation to the king before the action that follows.
segments.s047-g80: نشسته (participle/past form) + نهاده (past participle/verb 'placed') form a compound verbal phrase describing actions: 'having sat/while seated he placed'. به سر بر کلاه is a locative phrase with stacked prepositions: به سر 'on/onto the head' and بر کلاه 'on/over the hat'—i.e. he placed (it) on his head over the hat. The object is implied from context (the crown/ornament mentioned earlier).
segments.s047-g9: "که" here is a connective particle introducing the reported/result clause ('so/that'); the verb "فرستند" is past tense, 3rd‑person plural ('they sent') and appears before its object in this poetic word order. "سوی فریدون فرستند کس" therefore means 'they sent someone toward Fereydun' with "کس" an indefinite ('someone').
segments.s047-g10: "به پوزش" is a prepositional phrase 'for/unto pardon' ("به" = for/to; "پوزش" = pardon/excuse). "کجا" is a rhetorical adverb 'where (else)?' with the copular verb omitted (typical in poetic questions). "چاره این بود بس" inverts normal order: "این" (this) is the demonstrative subject, "بود" is the past copula 'was', and "بس" is a predicative/adverb meaning 'enough/ sufficient.' Together: 'Where else a remedy? This was enough.'
segments.s047-g11: "بجستند" is past tense plural from جستن ('they sought/they selected'); the phrase "از آن انجمن" ('from that assembly') is a prepositional noun phrase. "هردوان" ('both') follows the noun in classical/poetic order to mark that they selected both (i.e. two persons) from that gathering.
segments.s047-g12: "یکی" here means 'one (of them)'. "پاک‌دل" is a compound adjective 'pure‑hearted' placed before the noun "مرد" ('man'); "چیره‌زبان" is another adjectival compound 'master‑tongued' = 'eloquent' that characterizes the same man. The clause describes one of the chosen two as a kind‑hearted, eloquent man.
segments.s047-g13: The fused form "بدان" functions as "به + آن" = 'to that' (i.e. 'to that man'); here it introduces the indirect object. The nouns/adjectives "باهوش" (intelligent), "با رای" (literally 'with counsel', i.e. 'prudent/wise'), and "شرم" (modesty/modest) enumerate qualities of the man. Note the poetic use of "با + noun" to mean 'possessing/with X' (e.g. 'with counsel').
segments.s047-g14: "بگفتند" is past plural 'they said/spoke to him'. "با لابه" is an idiomatic phrase 'with entreaty/pleading' (here "لابه" means supplication — a poetic meaning not reflected in the literal dictionary senses shown). "بسیار گرم" qualifies the manner: "بسیار" = 'very/much' and "گرم" = 'fervent/ardent', so 'they begged him very warmly/earnestly.'
segments.s047-g15: "در گنج خاور" is a locative phrase 'in/at the eastern treasure (treasury)'; in poetry the genitive relation ('treasure of the east') is compacted. "گشادند" is past plural of 'گشودن/گشادن' here functioning as 'they opened' or 'they unlatched', and "باز" is an adverb 'again' (i.e. they opened it anew).
segments.s047-g16: "بدیدند" is past plural 'they saw'. The noun phrase "هول نشیب از فراز" groups as 'a terrifying descent from on high': "هول" = 'terror/terrifying', "نشیب" = 'a descent/sloping down', and "از فراز" = 'from the summit/above' ("فراز" = on high). The sequence is elliptical and compressed in the poetic style: they opened the vault and beheld a fearful fall/down‑slope coming from above.
segments.s047-g17: This is a compact, poetic noun phrase with a final verb. ز (preposition) introduces the source 'from the treasury'. The sequence گنج گهر تاج زر lists objects (treasure, jewel, crown, gold) in an inverted, paratactic order typical of epic diction, and خواستند (past, 3rd plural) is the main verb meaning 'they wanted/asked for/sought' referring back to the listed items.
segments.s047-g18: همی is a poetic continuous/emphatic particle here (marking ongoing or habitual action). پشت پیلان is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the backs of the elephants' (پشت + plural پیلان), and بیاراستند is the past, 3rd plural of a causative/decorative verb meaning 'they adorned/arrayed'. The clause therefore states the action performed on the elephants' backs.
segments.s047-g19: به گردونه‌ها بر uses two locative particles in succession (به ... بر) in a poetic/archaic stacking to mean 'upon/to the chariots'. چه is an exclamatory/eminence-marker introducing an itemized list ('what ...'), and مشک و عبیر are two scented substances listed in apposition. The verb is omitted here (elliptical) and is supplied from the surrounding context ('they placed/arrayed').
segments.s047-g20: This is the continuation of the enumerative exclamation (repeating چه for emphasis). The nouns are coordinated by و (and) in a simple list (دیبا, دینار, خز, حریر). There is no verb inside this segment; the list appends to the previous locative phrase and relies on the same understood action (they placed/arrayed these things).
segments.s047-g21: This segment begins with ابا, which in the manuscript sense-listing does not match a clear preposition here (it is not the standard با 'with'); none of the provided senses fit the particle's use, so its index is left undefined. پیل گردونکش is a noun phrase where پیل 'elephant' is modified by گردونکش ('upturner/overturner of the sky/heaven')—an epic epithet. The following و connects the coordinated nominal adjuncts رنگ و بوی ('colour and scent'), all serving as means/accompaniments for the ensuing motion.
segments.s047-g22: ز خاور به ایران sets the directional phrase: ز ('from') + خاور ('the East') and then به ایران ('toward Iran'). نهادند is the past, 3rd plural of نهادن 'to place/set' (here idiomatically 'to set one's face/turn toward'), and روی ('face') is the object of the idiom نهادنِ رویِ + به, meaning 'to direct/set (one's) face toward'.
segments.s047-g23: هر آنکس که is a universal relative 'everyone who/anyone who'. بد here is adverbial 'badly/poorly' (or 'ill' in the sense of 'in a bad state') modifying the verb phrase that follows; بر در شهریار is a locative phrase 'to/at the king's gate' (بر ... در used poetically for 'at the gate of the king'). The clause functions as the protasis (if/when) describing persons who approach the royal court.
segments.s047-g24: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' or 'each and every'. فرستادشان is past, 3rd singular (he sent them) in which the clitic -شان marks the object 'them'. یادگار is a noun 'memento/keepsake' and here names the manner or result of the sending (i.e. 'sent them as a memento'). The clause is syntactically the apodosis: what was done to such people.
segments.s047-g169: دو شاه دو کشور: a doubled noun phrase in apposition — 'two kings (of) two countries/realms' — where دو quantifies each noun. نشسته is a predicative/participial adjective 'seated/sitting'. به راز is a prepositional phrase 'in secret' (به + راز) modifying نشسته.
segments.s047-g170: بگفتند is past plural 'they said' introducing reported speech. کامد is an archaic/simple past/perfect-type verb 'came/has come' placed before its noun (classical word order); فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger'. باز is an adverb 'again/back' — here indicating the messenger's return.
segments.s047-g171: بیامد is 'he/it came' (past). هم آنگاه is a temporal/adverbial cluster ('also/and then' / 'then as well') that links this arrival to the previous action. سالار بار is a nominal phrase; سالار 'commander/chief' and بار 'load/burden' together indicate the officer in charge of the baggage or the commander who carried/was charged with the load.
segments.s047-g172: فرستاده را برد: فرستاده is the definite object 'the messenger' and را is the definite-object marker. برد here is past of بردن 'took/brought' ('brought' in context). زی شهریار is a short prepositional phrase meaning 'before/to the king' (زی is an archaic preposition indicating relation to شهریار).
segments.s047-g173: نشستنگهی نو is a noun phrase 'a new sitting/assembly-place' (نشستنگهی = place/occasion of sitting or assembly; نو = new). بیاراستند is past plural 'they adorned/arranged/prepared' and takes the preceding phrase as its object.
segments.s047-g174: ز شاه نو: ز 'from' (poetic), شاه نو 'the new king' (genitive/source). آیین خبر is a genitive-like phrase 'news concerning the ritual/ceremony' (آیین = ritual/ceremony, خبر = news). خواستند is past plural 'they requested/asked for' (here 'asked for news'). Word order places the source 'from the new king' before the object 'news of the ritual' and the verb.
segments.s047-g175: بجستند is past plural 'they sought/looked for'. هر گونه‌ای آگهی is the object phrase 'information/notice of every kind' (هر گونه‌ای = every kind/any sort; آگهی = information/notice). The verb precedes the object here, a common classical order.
segments.s047-g176: ز دیهیم و از تخت شاهنشهی: two parallel prepositional sources 'from the dihim' and 'from the throne of kings/empire'. ز/از both mean 'from' (poetic and standard forms). دیهیم refers to the rural people/countryside or villagers, تخت is 'throne' and شاهنشهی is an adjective/noun meaning 'imperial/royal' (i.e., the imperial court/throne).
segments.s047-g25: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause. پردخته‌شان is پردخته + the enclitic possessive ش (their) and functions as a past/participial adjective 'made/formed/ready'; شد is the simple past 'became/was' and yields a past-state reading. دل is the logical subject 'heart' placed after شد by poetic word order, and از خواسته ('from/despite desire') gives the cause or condition. Overall: 'When their hearts became/ were made ready from desire...'.
segments.s047-g26: فرستاده is the noun 'messenger' (subject); آمد is the simple past 'came'. برآراسته is a past/adjectival form 'well‑adorned' or 'neatly arrayed' functioning predicatively after the verb. The sequence is typical poetic inversion but reads simply as 'the messenger came, adorned.'
segments.s047-g27: بدادند is a past-tense plural verb 'they gave/delivered'; نزد + proper name marks location/presence 'before/in the presence of Fereydun'. پیام is the direct object 'message'. Word order here places the verb first, then the prepositional phrase (نزد فریدون), then the object, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s047-g28: نخست is an adverb 'first/firstly'. از جهاندار is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the sovereign' indicating source or reference. بردند (past plural) 'they carried/took/bore' is the verb and نام 'name' is its object; the clause means 'first they bore/took the name (of the sovereign)'.
segments.s047-g29: که introduces the following benedictory clause. جاوید is the adjective 'eternal'. باد here is the optative particle 'may' (a fixed literary formula), so 'جاوید باد' = 'may (he) be eternal'. آفریدون is the name; گرد 'around/about' modifies the scope ('throughout' or 'everywhere'). The clause thus expresses a wish: 'May Fereydun be eternal throughout.'
segments.s047-g30: همه فرهی is the object phrase 'all (forms of) nobility/excellence'. ایزد is the subject 'God/Providence' (here best translated 'Providence' or 'God' in a benedictive sense). او را uses the pronoun with the accusative/definite marker را 'him' as recipient, and سپرد is the past-tense verb 'entrusted/granted'. In the context of the preceding optative, this past form functions as a wish/blessing: 'may Providence grant him all nobility.' Note the slightly inverted order (object before subject) typical of poetry.
segments.s047-g31: سرش and تنش are nouns with the enclitic possessive ش 'his' (سر+ش, تن+ش). سرش سبز باد is an optative clause: سبز (predicate adjective) + باد (optative 'may') = 'may his head be green' (an idiomatic blessing meaning long life/fortune). و connects to the second coordinated item تنش ارجمند, where باد is omitted but understood; ارجمند is a predicate adjective 'esteemed/precious'.
segments.s047-g32: منش is 'character/lineage'. برگذشته is a compound meaning 'surpassed/going beyond' or 'having passed over'; ز چرخ بلند is a prepositional phrase 'from the high sky' — چرخ in epic Persian often means 'fate/sky/heaven' and here carries a lofty/cosmic sense. The whole phrase is a poetic wish/invocation: 'may his character surpass the high heavens.' Note absence of an explicit copula—predicate relations are shown by word order and case particles.
segments.s047-g153: Phrase is SOV: the noun phrase 'فرستاده آن هول گفتار' (literally 'the messenger that‑terrifying speech') is the object(s)liciting the verb 'دید' at the end. 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that/the', 'هول' (literally 'terror') functions adjectivally modifying 'گفتار' ('speech, utterance'), and 'دید' is the perfective past 'saw/ beheld'.
segments.s047-g154: 'نشست' here is a noun meaning 'session/sitting' (not the verb 'to sit'); the line continues with another past verb 'دید' at the end. 'منوچهر سالار' is a titled name phrase (Manuchehr the chief/sovereign). Word order is again (object) + (name) + verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s047-g155: 'بپژمرد' is a perfective past verb meaning 'he withered/grew pale' (the initial b- is the classical simple past prefix). 'و' links the two actions, and 'برخاست' is another past verb 'stood up/rose'. 'لرزان' is an adverbial/participial adjective 'trembling' modifying the subject, and 'ز جای' = 'from his place' (ز = 'from', جای = 'place').
segments.s047-g156: 'هم آنگه' is a conjunctive adverbial cluster meaning roughly 'and then / likewise then'. The verb 'آورد' is past 'brought/put', with its object 'پای' ('foot') following in the postpositional phrase 'به زین اندر آورد پای' = 'he put his foot into the saddle' (به/اندر = towards/into). Word order is slightly marked (verb before its direct object) as is common in epic poetry.
segments.s047-g157: 'همه بودنیها' is the object 'all beings/things that are' (همه = all; بودنیها = those that exist). 'به روشن‌روان' is an adverbial phrase 'with a clear/bright soul' (به here functions as an instrumental or adverbial marker 'with/by/with a ...'), modifying the manner of perception.
segments.s047-g158: The verb 'بدید' (past 'saw') precedes the subject phrase 'آن گرانمایه مرد جوان' — a common poetic inversion (V before S). 'آن' = that/the, 'گرانمایه' = precious/noble, and 'مرد جوان' = 'young man' (مرد = man; جوان adjectival 'young').
segments.s047-g159: 'که' is the relative/connector 'that/who/for', linking this clause to the preceding description. 'با سلم و با تور' names two companions or persons (Salm and Tur) introduced by the comitative 'با' repeated for emphasis. 'گردان سپهر' is a genitive/adjectival phrase 'the revolving/turning sky' (گردان modifying سپهر), literally 'the sky that turns'.
segments.s047-g160: This clause expresses a negative temporal extent: 'نه بس دیر' = 'not for very long / not long', where 'نه' negates and 'بس' intensifies. 'چین' = 'wrinkle(s)' is the grammatical subject, 'اندر آرد' = 'brings upon' (آرد = present/past classical 'brings'), and 'بچهر' = 'to his face' (ب- prefix + چهر 'face' forming 'to/onto the face'). Word order is slightly inverted (subject/phrase + verb + target).
segments.s047-g41: نوشته (past participle) + چنین (adverb) + بودمان: here the past participle نوشته ('written') is followed by چنین ('thus'), and بودمان is the past auxiliary بود with enclitic مان ('to/for us'), meaning 'had been (to us)' — together: 'it had been written to us thus'.
segments.s047-g42: به رسم بوش is a prepositional phrase 'in/according to the custom of Būsh' (به + رسم + proper name), and اندر آمد روش is a verbal phrase where اندر آمد ('came into/appeared') governs روش ('manner/way'), i.e. 'a way/manner came into being.'
segments.s047-g43: This is a nominal phrase listing two powerful figures: هژبر is a proper name/epithet, جهانسوز is an epithet 'world‑consuming' modifying it, and then و نر اژدها adds 'and the male dragon' (نر = male, اژدها = dragon).
segments.s047-g44: ز here is the poetic/prepositional 'from'; 'ز دام قضا' = 'from the snare/trap of fate'; هم 'even/also' emphasizes inability; نیابد is a negative 3rd‑person form of یافتن meaning 'does not/finds not' (i.e. 'cannot find/obtain'); رها is predicate meaning 'release/free' — together: 'even from the snare of fate he cannot find release.'
segments.s047-g45: و دیگر که opens another item ('and another, that...'); فرمان ناپاک دیو is a genitive noun phrase 'the impure demon's command' (فرمان = command; ناپاک = impure/evil; دیو = demon) with ناپاک qualifying فرمان.
segments.s047-g46: ببرد is a 3rd‑person verb form from بردن 'to take/carry' (here 'carried off' / 'snatched away'); دل is the object 'heart' (emotion/mind); از introduces the cause/source 'from' or 'because of'; ترس گیهان خدیو = 'fear of the world's khedive' (ترس = fear; گیهان = world; خدیو = khedive/ruler) — literally 'the command of the impure demon carried away the heart because of fear of the world's khedive.'
segments.s047-g47: به ما بر is a classical locative/experiential construction 'befell us' (به + ما + بر conveying 'upon us'); چنین is 'thus/so'; خیره شد is a compound meaning 'became amazed/stunned' or 'fixed (in astonishment)'; رای بد is the noun phrase 'bad/evil counsel/plan' that is the thing which has struck/befallen — overall: 'such a bad counsel befell us, leaving us stunned.'
segments.s047-g48: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'so that/that'; مغز دو فرزند is a genitive construction 'the brains/minds of the two sons' (مغز = brain/core used for mind); شد is 'became'; جای بد 'a bad place/seat' — 'so that the minds of the two sons became an evil seat' (i.e. their minds became corrupt/ill‑disposed).
segments.s047-g89: This is a temporal clause introduced by چون (here functioning like 'when'). The subject فرستاده (‘the messenger’) comes first, then the verb دید (past 'saw') and its object درگاه شاه (‘the king's court’). Classical word order allows the subordinate temporal marker چو/چون before the verb, yielding 'when the messenger saw the king's court.'
segments.s047-g90: This string gives manner and motion: پیاده دوان is a compound adverbial phrase ('on foot, running'), followed by the verb اندر آمد (an older/formal 'entered/came in'). The prepositional phrase ز راه ('from the road') appears after the verb in classical order. دوان functions adverbially (running), not as a main finite verb.
segments.s047-g91: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; نزدیک شاه آفریدون ('near King Afrīdūn') is an adverbial phrase of approach. رسید is the past tense 'reached/came near.' The subject is carried over from the previous clause (the messenger), so this reads 'when he came near King Afrīdūn.'
segments.s047-g92: A list of direct objects joined by و ('and'): سر (head), تخت (throne), و تاج (and crown). The adjective بلند ('lofty/high') follows and is attached to the enclitic ش (his), so بلندش = 'his lofty.' The verb بدید ('saw') is final (past tense), so literally 'he saw head and throne and his lofty crown.'
segments.s047-g93: ز بالا فرو برد is a classical verbal phrase meaning 'lowered/bowed down from above' (فروبرد is a compound perfective). سر ('head') is the object, and پیش اوی ('before him' / 'in his presence') contains the preposition پیش plus the enclitic اوی = 'him.' The clause thus means 'he bowed his head down before him.'
segments.s047-g94: همی is an archaic/emphatic marker often giving iterative or immediate sense; here it precedes the action 'he pressed/rubbed.' بر زمین ('upon the ground') gives the location, then بر بمالید (past 'rubbed/pressed') and روی ('his face') is the patient/object at the end. The doubled بر is idiomatic emphasis in older Persian.
segments.s047-g95: This is a stacked nominal phrase of titles: گرانمایه ('esteemed/noble') modifies شاه جهان ('king of the world'), and کدخدای is an additional epithet/appellation. The phrase names or addresses the monarch with honorifics; there is no verb here until the next segment.
segments.s047-g96: به کرسی زرین ('to/onto the golden seat') is the goal phrase, with ورا an older enclitic variant of 'او را' meaning 'to him.' کرد (past of کردن) is the main verb 'made/did' and جای ('place') is the object: 'made a place for him on the golden seat' = 'placed him on the golden throne.' The order is classical: goal phrase + pronoun + verb + noun.
segments.s047-g201: خرامان شدم is a two-word intransitive past construction: خرامان is an adverb/adverbial adjective 'with a proud/stately gait' and شدم is the past of شدن used here to mean 'I became / I began to (walk)' — together functioning as 'I strode / I walked proudly.' پیش آن ارجمند places the action spatially/relationally: پیش = 'in the presence of / before,' آن 'that,' and ارجمند 'esteemed, noble.' The order is topical (action then the prepositional phrase naming before whom).
segments.s047-g202: This clause is a simple SOV-style clause with the verb دیدم 'I saw' at the end. یکی is the indefinite/numeral 'a certain / one' introducing the noun phrase یکی تخت پیروزه 'a single / a certain turquoise throne.' بلند is a postpositive adjective 'high / tall' modifying تخت; Persian frequently places adjectives after the noun.
segments.s047-g203: نشسته is the adjectival/past participle 'seated' (here functioning as a stative predicate). شهریاری is a noun meaning 'sovereignty / kingship' used adjectivally to describe the figure's royal character; the construction برو شهریاری is an archaic fixed phrase meaning 'majestic/regal' (not the modern imperative 'go'). چو ماه is a simile 'like the moon.' Word order puts the state (نشسته) before the quality/description.
segments.s047-g204: This prepositional/attributive cluster describes head-ornamentation: ز + یاقوت رخشان = 'radiant from/of ruby' (ز is the poetic 'from/of'), followed by a locative phrase به سر بر کلاه where بر + کلاه = 'upon a cap/hat' and به سر helps mark 'on the head.' The whole phrase means roughly 'with a ruby shining on his head as a cap.'
segments.s047-g205: چو introduces similes: چو کافور موی = 'hair like camphor' (کافور, camphor, used as a whiteness/brightness comparison), و joins the parallel simile چو گلبرگ روی = 'and face like a petal.' Adjectival/image words follow the nouns they modify, typical in classical Persian poetic diction.
segments.s047-g206: دل آزرم جوی is a compact phrase: دل 'heart' + آزرم (here treated as a proper name or fixed word) + جوی 'seeking' (جوی as a noun/participle meaning 'searching/seek'). و links to زبان چرب‌گوی where زبان is 'tongue / speech' and چرب‌گوی is a compound adjective 'smooth-tongued / flattering.' The adjective follows the noun it modifies, and جوی is used in a participial/agent sense 'one that seeks' or 'seeking.'
segments.s047-g207: جهان را uses the definite object/topic marker را to front and emphasize 'the world.' ازو 'from/of him' gives a causal/associative sense ('because of him / on his account'). دل is 'the heart' and به بیم و امید is a prepositional phrase 'in fear and hope' describing the heart's condition. The clause stacks topical/object marking before the descriptive phrase, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s047-g208: تو is the 2nd-person singular subject 'you.' گفتی here functions as 'you said' (past tense), though the provided sense list doesn't show the verbal sense — it is the past of گفتن. مگر introduces a rhetorical question meaning 'could it be that / is it that...?' زنده شد is the past 'became alive / came back to life' (زنده 'alive' + شد 'became'), and جمشید is the proper name. Order is subject – verb – interrogative clause in reported speech.
segments.s047-g49: همی + چشم داریم = a fixed idea: 'همی' (still/always) + 'چشم داشتن به کسی' is an idiom meaning 'to look to / depend on someone.' Word order is literal: 'we have our eye' (i.e. we keep expecting) 'از آن تاجور' — 'از' here marks the person one looks to (classical usage). 'تاجور' is a noun 'merchant/trader' used as the person relied upon.
segments.s047-g50: The clause begins with the relative/complementizer 'که' introducing the hoped-for action. 'بخشایش' means 'pardon/forgiveness' (not merely generosity). 'آرد' is the classical 3rd-person present/optative form 'he may bring / he brings' (Ferdowsi often uses this older form). 'به ما' marks the beneficiary 'to us'; 'بر' is the preposition 'upon / to' following the verb in older ordering ('آرد بر ما' = 'grant to us'). 'مگر' here is a modal particle used like 'perhaps / maybe' (poetic usage) rather than its more literal meanings.
segments.s047-g51: 'اگر چه' is the concessive 'although / even though' (here 'اگر' + the particle 'چه' forming a concessive phrase). 'بزرگست' is an older fused form of 'بزرگ است' = 'is great' (present). 'ما را گناه' uses the object/topic marker 'را' after 'ما' to mark 'our' / 'us' as possessor/object in classical order; together it reads 'our sin' or literally 'the sin of us.'
segments.s047-g52: 'به بی‌دانشی' is an ablative/agentive phrase 'by/through ignorance' — note 'به' here functions like the English 'by' (classical usage). 'برنهد' is the classical present/infinitive form of 'بستن/نهادن' meaning 'to place / to set upon' (here 'he places/sets forth'). 'پیشگاه' means 'the presence (of the court/king)' so the whole clause means '(he) puts [it] before the court because of ignorance.'
segments.s047-g53: This is a nominal list: 'و دیگر بهانه' = 'and another pretext / excuse.' 'سپهر بلند' is a noun + adjective in classical order: 'the lofty sky' or 'high firmament.' Word order is straightforward: 'another pretext: the lofty sky.'
segments.s047-g54: 'که' introduces a descriptive clause: 'which' or 'so that.' 'گاهی ... و گاهی ...' is a common correlative pair meaning 'sometimes ... and sometimes ...'. 'پناهست' is the fused form 'پناه است' = 'is a refuge' (present), and 'گزند' means 'harm' or 'injury.' So the clause says the sky is at times a refuge and at times harmful.
segments.s047-g55: This is an older/poetic clause: 'سوم' = 'third,' introducing the third item. 'دیو' = 'demon.' 'کاندر' is an archaic fused form meaning 'in which / wherein' or 'therein' (here functioning to locate the demon 'in the midst'). 'میان' = 'middle / midst.' 'چون' = 'like / as.' 'نوند' is treated as a name/term (here compared to 'نوند' — a poetic reference). The clause describes 'the third [is] the demon which, in the midst, like Nund...' (elliptical, as is typical in verse).
segments.s047-g56: 'میان بسته دارد' = 'he keeps the middle closed' (بسته = closed; دارد = he has/keeps) — an idiomatic way to say he shuts off the center. 'ز بهر' = classical 'for the sake of / to ward off' (here 'ز' = from, 'بهر' = for), and 'گزند' = 'harm.' So the clause means 'he keeps the middle closed in order to prevent/guard against harm.'
segments.s047-g97: Subject 'فرستاده' (the envoy) comes first, followed by the prepositional phrase 'بر شاه' (toward/to the king). The light verb phrase 'کرد آفرین' places the verb 'کرد' (did/made) before its noun object 'آفرین' (an acclamation), a common poetic word order meaning 'praised' or 'exclaimed praise.'
segments.s047-g98: The clause begins with the complementizer 'که' (here 'that') introducing what was said. 'نازش' is 'ناز' + pronominal suffix 'ش' = 'his/its nâz' (here meaning 'his pride/delight'), and the list 'تاج و تخت و نگین' is a simple coordinated noun phrase (crown and throne and jewel) serving as the predicate/content of that clause.
segments.s047-g99: This is a poetic copular clause: 'زمین گلشن' (the earth a garden) with the reason/ground given by the prepositional phrase 'از پایه تخت' ('from/because of the base/foundation of the throne'). The final word 'تست' is a terse, archaic/poetic copula (literally written as 'تست') functioning like 'توست' = 'is/of you' (here indicating the throne belongs to you), so the clause links the world’s being a garden to your throne.
segments.s047-g100: 'زمان روشن' is a noun + adjective ('the time/age bright'); 'از مایه بخت' is another causal phrase ('from the source/cause of fortune'). As above, the final 'تست' functions as an archaic copular form (poetic 'توست') connecting the brightness of the age to the source of the addressee's fortune.
segments.s047-g101: 'همه' here is an adverbial/intensifying modifier ('wholly/entirely') modifying the predicate that follows. The phrase 'بنده خاک پای' is a fused nominal expression 'servant (at) the dust of (your) foot', a humble formulaic self-designation. 'توایم' is an old fused present ('تو' + 'ام') meaning 'I am yours' or 'I belong to you.'
segments.s047-g102: Parallel to the previous clause: 'همه' again intensifies ('wholly'), followed by two predicative adjectives 'پاک زنده' ('pure, alive'). The prepositional phrase 'به رای' ('by/at the counsel/will') modifies how the speaker exists, and the final 'توایم' again is the archaic fused 'I am yours/at your command.'
segments.s047-g103: 'پیام دو خونی' is a noun phrase with the adjective 'دو خونی' ('blood‑red' or 'two‑blooded/bloody') modifying 'پیام' (message/news). The periphrastic inchoative construction 'به گفتن گرفت' uses 'به' + infinitive + 'گرفت' to mean 'began to be said' or 'took to saying' — 'گرفت' is a perfective main verb here used as an auxiliary indicating onset.
segments.s047-g104: Similarly, 'همه راستیها' ('all the truths') is the subject, and 'نهفتن گرفت' repeats the inchoative pattern: infinitive 'نهفتن' (to hide/conceal) with 'گرفت' (took/began) to mean 'began to be hidden' or 'were put into concealment.'
segments.s047-g177: ز is the poetic short form of از meaning 'from'; ز شاه آفریدون = 'from King Afrīdūn'. The conjunction و + از introduces a parallel phrase: و از لشکرش = 'and from his army', where the possessive suffix -ش on لشکر marks 'his'. Word order is preposition + noun, then conjunction + preposition + possessed noun.
segments.s047-g178: ز again = 'from' introduces a new parallel item: ز گردان جنگی = 'from a troop/company (گردان) that is warlike (جنگی)'; the adjective جنگی follows the noun. و از کشورش repeats the pattern 'and from his country/realm' with -ش marking possession. The repetition of ز/از is a poetic listing device.
segments.s047-g179: و دیگر = 'and further/again' (دیگر = 'another/more'). ز governs the whole noun phrase that follows: ز کردار گردان سپهر = 'from the deeds/workings (کردار) of the revolving (گردان) heaven/sky (سپهر)'; here گردان is the adjectival sense 'revolving', modifying سپهر.
segments.s047-g180: که introduces a relative clause 'which' referring back to 'گردان سپهر'. دارد is the classical present 'has/possesses'; همی is an archaic continuous particle meaning 'always/continually' and reinforces the ongoing sense of دارد. بر + noun marks the target of the affection: بر منوچهر مهر = 'has love/affection toward Manūchehr.' Word order is relative pronoun → verb → adverb → preposition → object → noun.
segments.s047-g181: بزرگان کدامند = 'who/which are the great ones' (کدامند is the interrogative + copula form 'are they'). و دستور کیست = 'and whose is the command/order?' (کیست = 'who is it' used to ask about possession or attribution). The two clauses are coordinate questions.
segments.s047-g182: چه = 'what'; مایستشان is an archaic fused form meaning roughly 'what is theirs' or 'what befits them / what is their standing'. The phrase lists گنج و گنجور = 'treasure and treasury' as the things in question, followed by کیست (copula interrogative) to ask 'who is (it)' or 'who possesses/controls them'. The word order is poetic/elliptical but functions as 'What is their treasure and treasury, and who is (it)?'.
segments.s047-g183: فرستاده گفت is the reporting clause 'the messenger said' (گفت here reports speech). آنکه = 'he who' (relative pronoun) begins the reported clause; روشن بهار = 'the bright/clear spring', with روشن modifying بهار.
segments.s047-g184: بدید is the past simple 'saw'; و links it to ببیند, which here is a present/habitual form 'sees' (classical present with بـ-). The object 'the bright spring' carries over from the previous segment; در شهریار = 'in the king/sovereign' (locative), so the clause means 'he saw (the spring) and now sees (it) in the king.'
segments.s047-g105: This is a noun phrase describing a person: the compound adjective گشاده‌زبان (placed before the noun) modifies مرد (the head noun). بسیار qualifies هوش that follows, so هوش here is an abstract noun meaning 'intellect/wit' and 'بسیار هوش' means 'of great wit' (classical Persian often strings modifiers without ezāfe).
segments.s047-g106: The verbal phrase is elliptical/compact: داده is the past participle used as the past verb 'gave/lent', بدو is the indirect object pronoun 'to him', شاه جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign king', and گوش is an abstract object meaning 'ear/attention' (i.e. 'to lend an ear'). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'بدو داده' (gave to him) appears before the subject.
segments.s047-g107: ز here is the poetic preposition 'because of/from'; کردار بد is the noun phrase 'bad deed/behavior'; پوزش آراستن is an infinitival collocation meaning 'to offer/prepare an apology' (آراستن takes پوزش as its object). The whole phrase functions as the reason clause for what follows.
segments.s047-g108: منوچهر را: منوچهر is the definite object marked by را. نزد خود is a locative phrase 'to his presence / to himself' (نزد = 'in the presence of'); خواستن here is a transitive verb meaning 'to call/summon' or 'to request someone to come' (used in the narrative past sense).
segments.s047-g109: میان بستن is a compound verbal idea meaning 'to settle/make peace' or 'to conclude between parties' with او را as its object ('to reconcile with him'). بسان رهی is a simile 'as one treats a traveler' that specifies the manner. Note classical ordering: verb phrase plus object plus manner-simile.
segments.s047-g110: سپردن is the verb 'to entrust/hand over'; بدو is the indirect object 'to him'; تاج و تخت مهی is the direct object 'the crown and the moon-like throne', with مهی (literally 'moon-like') as a poetic adjective qualifying تخت. The infinitive form here represents the narrated past action of handing over.
segments.s047-g111: خریدن ازو باز خون پدر is an infinitival clause 'to buy back from him the father's blood' (خریدن = 'to buy/redeem', ازو = 'from him', باز = adverb 'back/again', خون پدر = 'the father's blood', i.e. ransom or compensation). The verb expresses the act of redeeming blood/avelengement.
segments.s047-g112: This is a list of the consideration paid: بدینار ('for a dinar' — indicating payment), followed by conjunctions و joining the goods (دیبا 'brocade', تاج 'crown', کمر 'belt/girdle'). Classical Persian often strings such items with و between them; کمر here denotes a girdle/belt accessory.
segments.s047-g209: منوچهر is the subject (the king's name). چون here introduces the time of birth (classical/poetic use = “when”/“as”), followed by the past form زاد (here meaning ‘was born’ / ‘came into being’). سرو بلند is a predicative simile placed after the verb: سرو = ‘cypress’ and بلند = ‘tall’ describing his stature at birth.
segments.s047-g210: به کردار is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in the manner/character of’ (به = in, کردار = conduct/behavior). طهمورث دیوبند is a name-title unit: طهمورث (Tahmuras) followed by the epithet دیوبند (here a traditional epithet attached to Tahmuras). The whole phrase qualifies Manuchehr’s character.
segments.s047-g211: نشسته is a past participle/adjectival verb meaning ‘seated’ (state/resultative). بر شاه بر دست راست is a nested locative: بر = on/at, شاه = the king, then بر دست راست = ‘at the right hand’ (دست = hand, راست = right). The doubled بر frames the exact position ‘at the king’s right hand.’
segments.s047-g212: تو گویی is an idiomatic, mildly poetic expression meaning ‘one would say’ or ‘it seems (as if)’ (تو here is used impersonally/colloquially rather than addressing a specific you). زبان و دل is a simple coordinate (‘tongue/speech and heart’). پادشاست is a predicative expression meaning ‘are (of) the king’ / ‘belong to the king’ (here attributive/possessive idea: they serve the king).
segments.s047-g213: به پیش اندرون is a locative sequence: به + پیش = ‘at/in front of’ and اندرون = ‘the inner place/inner court.’ قارن رزم زن is a name followed by a descriptive epithet: قارن (proper name) and رزم زن literally ‘battle‑striker’ = ‘warrior’ (رزم = battle, زن here = one who strikes/fights).
segments.s047-g214: به دست چپش is another locative: به = at/to, دست = hand, چپش = his left (چپ + ـش possessive). سرو شاه یمن strings the epithets: سرو = ‘cypress’ (used metaphorically for a tall or stately person), شاه = king, یمن = Yemen (here ‘the Yemeni king’); the whole phrase places that ‘cypress’ at his left hand.
segments.s047-g215: چو begins a comparative clause meaning ‘as’ or ‘just as.’ شاه یمن سرو is a genitive/attributive chain meaning ‘the cypress of the king of Yemen’ (شاه یمن = king of Yemen, سرو = cypress). دستورشان combines دستور (‘order/command’ implying commander or director here) with the third‑person plural possessive ـشان = ‘their’ — ‘their commander/one who gives orders.’
segments.s047-g216: The second چو parallels the first (‘so’ / ‘just as’). پیروز is an adjective/noun ‘victorious’ and modifies the name گرشاسپ (Gershasp, legendary hero). گنجورشان (گنجور + ـشان) means ‘their treasurer/treasury‑holder’ (literally related to ‘treasure/treasury’ with a possessive), completing the parallel: as X is their commander, so Y is their treasurer.
segments.s047-g185: This is a nominal clause with the old copula 'ست' (= است) after the predicate: 'بهاری ست' = 'it is springlike/it is spring'. Word order is adjective + copula + adjective/adverbial phrase ('خرم در اردیبهشت') giving the time/place. 'در اردیبهشت' is a prepositional phrase ('in Ordibehesht').
segments.s047-g186: This is a verbless, adjectival/equative poetic clause: 'همه خاک عنبر' = 'the whole earth [is] amber(‑scented)'. The repetition pattern 'همه ... همه ...' links two parallel nominal phrases; the copula is omitted (typical in poetry).
segments.s047-g187: 'سپهر برین' uses the suffix-like element 'ین' (here forming برین = 'exalted/lofty'), so 'سپهر برین' = 'the lofty sky/heavens'. The structure 'X اوست' ('کاخ و میدان اوست') is a nominal sentence: 'the palace and the square are his.' The copula is the archaic 'ست'.
segments.s047-g188: Parallel nominal clause: 'بهشت برین' ('the highest/most exalted paradise') followed by the predicate phrase 'روی خندان اوست' where 'روی' (face) + adjective 'خندان' describes the subject and 'اوست' is the copula ('is his'). Word order is noun phrase + adjectival modifier + copula.
segments.s047-g189: 'به بالای ایوان او' is a locative prepositional phrase ('on the top of his portico'). The predicate 'راغ نیست' is negative existential: 'there is no رغ (longing/desire)'. Note the clitic possession 'او' follows the possessed noun (ایوان او = his portico).
segments.s047-g190: Similar construction: 'به پهنای میدان او' = 'across the breadth/width of his square' (prepositional phrase) followed by 'باغ نیست' = 'there is no garden.' Again negative existential; word order places the locative phrase fronted for emphasis (poetic inverted order).
segments.s047-g191: 'چو رفتم' = 'when I went/when I came' (چو = 'when/as', رفتم = 1st person past of رفتن). The rest 'به نزدیک ایوان فراز' is an adverbial phrase ('near the lofty/raised portico'), where 'فراز' conveys 'on high/raised' and modifies ایوان.
segments.s047-g192: 『سرش با ستاره همی گفت راز』: 'سرش' = 'his head' (سر + possessive clitic ش). 'با ستاره' = 'with a star'; the particle 'همی' marks a continuous or ongoing action in older/poetic Persian (here 'was ...ing'). 'گفت راز' = 'spoke/uttered a secret' (past tense of گفتن). Overall: 'his head was speaking/whispering a secret with a star.'
segments.s047-g217: شمار is the noun 'number/tally' and functions as the subject/topic. در گنجها is a locative prepositional phrase ('in/among the treasures') that specifies where the number applied. ناپدید is a past participle/adjectival predicate 'vanished/gone' used without an explicit copula (a common poetic construction), so the clause reads literally 'the number among the treasures (was) vanished.'
segments.s047-g218: کس here combines with the negative past verb ندید to mean 'no one.' اندر is a locative preposition 'in/within,' modifying جهان 'the world.' آن بزرگی is a demonstrative + noun 'that greatness.' ندید is the past tense of دیدن with the negative prefix (did-not-see) — i.e. 'saw not,' giving the natural sense 'no one in the world saw such greatness.'
segments.s047-g219: همه گرد is an adverbial phrase 'all around.' ایوان is the place (iwan/portico) being encircled. دو رویه سپاه is a nominal phrase describing the army as arranged in 'two faces/ranks' (an epithet placed after the noun in poetic word order). The whole clause presents the scene: 'all around the iwan (stood) the two-ranked host.'
segments.s047-g220: به is used instrumentally/associatively here ('with' / 'by means of'). زرین modifies the following noun as an adjective 'golden.' عمود means 'pillar/column.' و is the coordinating conjunction 'and.' The second به ... زرین ... کلاه parallels the first phrase: 'with golden pillars and with golden helmets.' The construction links architectural decoration and martial gear by parallel prepositional phrases.
segments.s047-g221: سپهدار is the noun 'commander' (subject). چو introduces a simile 'like/as.' قارن کاوگان is an appositive phrase: a (proper) name plus an epithet; in context it identifies the commander by name/epithet. No finite verb is required here for the poetic nominative phrase.
segments.s047-g222: به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'in front of / before.' سپاه is 'the army' (object of the preposition). اندرون is an adverb/noun meaning 'inside' or 'inner (quarters),' and آوگان is the plural 'voices / chanters' — together 'the inner chanters.' Word order places the locative phrase before the noun phrase to indicate that the chanters stood/acted before the army.
segments.s047-g223: مبارز is the noun 'fighter/warrior.' چو introduces a simile 'like/as.' شیروی is an adjectival form 'lion-like / brave' describing the fighter; درنده modifies شیر to form the compound 'درنده شیر' = 'a predatory/ferocious lion.' The sequence is appositional: the fighter is likened to a ferocious lion.
segments.s047-g224: چو again marks a simile 'as/like.' شاپور is a proper name (here an epithet of the fighter). یل is an appositional noun 'hero/brave man.' ژنده پیل is a compound epithet — ژنده (literally 'rag[-like]') and پیل 'elephant' used figuratively for size/strength; together they function as descriptive epithets. دلیر is a final adjective 'brave/courageous' summarizing the quality. The line strings several appositions after the simile to magnify the warrior.
segments.s047-g1: به سلم و به تور — a prepositional phrase with the preposition به repeated for emphasis (’to Salm and to Tur’). In classical Persian narrative the preposition precedes the proper names. آمد این آگهی — the verb آمد (past, 3rd pl./3rd sg. depending on sense) comes before the subject phrase این آگهی; poetic word order often places the verb before its subject.
segments.s047-g2: که شروع‌کنندهٔ جملهٔ وابسته است here meaning 'that' (introducing the content of the news). شد روشن — شد is perfective/past 'became' (copular/becoming), روشن is predicate adjective 'clear/manifest'; together 'became clear'. آن تخت شاهنشهی is the noun phrase (that/the imperial throne) serving as the theme whose state is described.
segments.s047-g3: دل هر دو بیدادگر — literal order 'heart of each of the two, the bīdādgar', with هر دو qualifying the two persons; بیدادگر here describes the two (’the two tyrants/cruel ones’). پر نهیب — پر is an adjective 'full (of)', followed by the noun نهیب 'a loud cry/clamor' (so 'full of a thunderous cry'). This is a compact poetic nominal clause describing their inner state.
segments.s047-g4: که here is causal ('for/because'). اختر همی رفت — همی is the archaic/prosodic present-continuous particle found in classical Persian (marks ongoing action), and رفت is the past/preterite of 'to go' used here in narrative to mean 'was moving' or 'proceeded'. سوی نشیب is a prepositional phrase 'toward the descent/decline' (an astrological or metaphorical decline).
segments.s047-g5: نشستند is the past plural verb 'they sat down' (note: the lemma/sense list given is mismatched, so this item is treated as the verb 'to sit'). هر دو follows the verb for emphasis ('both of them'), and به اندیشگان uses به + noun to indicate purpose or state ('into thought' / 'for deliberation'); اندیشگان is a noun form meaning 'thought/deliberation' or 'the thoughtful ones' in older usage, here functioning as the object/goal of sitting.
segments.s047-g6: شده is a perfective participle/auxiliary meaning 'having become' or 'became'. تیره‌روز is an adjective 'ill-fated, dark-dayed', and جفاپیشگان is an epithet 'oppressors/cruel ones' placed after it; the phrase means 'having become ill-fated, the oppressors' — a concise poetic appraisal of their changed condition.
segments.s047-g7: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each in turn' and is fronted for emphasis. بران = بر + آن (here 'on/with regard to that' or 'thereon/thereupon'), linking to the preceding situation. رایشان (رای + شان) 'their counsel/plan' is the topic/object; شد درست is a verbal phrase where شد means 'became / was made' and درست 'right/correct' — 'their counsel was made correct/settled.'
segments.s047-g8: کزان is a compound of که + از + آن (here 'from which / whence') introducing an adverbial cause or source. روی شان 'their face/standing' (روی here 'side/position' with the possessive شان) denotes the aspect from which action follows. چاره بایست جست is a fixed classical sequence: چاره = 'remedy/solution', بایست is a modal 'must/ought', and جست is an older infinitive/verb 'to seek' (here object of بایست); together 'a remedy must be sought.' Word order places the modal and verb at the end, as is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s047-g57: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). پادشا را uses را to mark 'the king' as the topical/affected noun of the coming verb. سر از کین ما is a noun phrase meaning 'head (i.e. mind/heart) from our hatred' — از marks separation ('free from') and ما is the possessor 'our'. The whole segment sets the condition 'if the king's head is freed from our hatred.'
segments.s047-g58: شود is the classical third-person verbal form 'become' (here indicating the resulting state). پاک و روشن are coordinate adjectives modifying the following noun (lit. 'pure and clear/illuminated'). The second شود applies to دین ما ('our religion') which is put after the adjectives by poetic inversion — overall: 'our religion will become pure and bright.'
segments.s047-g59: منوچهر را places Manuchehr as the direct object/topicalized person with را. با سپاه گران is an instrument/ accompaniment phrase 'with a heavy/weighty army' where گران qualifies سپاه.
segments.s047-g60: فرستد is the classical past/preterite form 'sent/dispatched.' به نزدیک is a goal/directional phrase 'toward/near,' and خواهشگران is the plural 'supplicants' or 'those who entreat' — together 'sent ... toward the supplicants.'
segments.s047-g61: بدان is the imperative/infinitival formation (ب- + دان) meaning 'know this/consider.' تا introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that'). چو here means 'when/if' and introduces a subordinate clause 'when (we are) a servant before him'; به پیشش is 'in his presence/before him' and به پای literally 'at (his) feet' (an idiom for humble subservience).
segments.s047-g62: بباشیم is a subjunctive/optative first-person-plural form of 'to be' meaning 'let us be/ may we be / we shall be.' جاوید is the predicate adjective 'eternal.' و links the clauses, and اینست رای is a formulaic clause 'this is the counsel/plan' (اینست = 'this is', رای = 'plan/counsel').
segments.s047-g63: مگر is used here as 'unless' (introducing an exception). کان is an archaic/deictic word ('that' / 'a certain') used to point to a particular thing; درختی is 'a tree' (indefinite/qualifying), کزین = کِز + این 'from this' and کین 'hatred' — together 'a tree from which this hatred came/grew.' برست is an archaic past form meaning 'sprang/rose/was produced' (poetic past of 'to arise').
segments.s047-g64: به آب دو دیده is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'with the water of the two eyes' (i.e. tears). توانیم is the present/subjunctive form of توانستن 'we can/are able to,' and شست is the stem/infinitival form of 'to wash' used after توانستن — 'we can wash (it).' Together: 'we can wash it with the water of our two eyes.'
segments.s047-g33: بدان کان is an archaic/discourse marker: بدان is an imperative/introductory verb ('know' or 'know then') built from the prefix ب- + دان(=know), and کان here is an old conjunction linking what follows ('that' / 'for it was that'). دو بدخواه بیدادگر is a noun phrase: numeral + noun + adjectival/noun epithet, literally 'two ill-wishers, tyrants' (بدخواه and بیدادگر stand in apposition, both following the numeral).
segments.s047-g34: پر از ... is the common construction 'full of X' (پر + از + noun). آب‌دیده here describes tearful, literally 'water‑seen' (eyes full of water/tears). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' and links شرم پدر ('shame before the father') as the cause. Word order: 'پر از [X] ز [Y]' = 'full of X because of Y.'
segments.s047-g35: پشیمان شده is a verbal adjective/periphrastic perfect meaning 'having become remorseful' (شده = become). داغ دل is a compound noun 'the heart's burning' = deep grief or anguish; بر گناه uses the preposition بر in the sense 'over/about' to mark the cause/object of that grief ('over their sin').
segments.s047-g36: همی is a classical verbal prefix/adverb meaning continuing/habitual (often 'still' or a simple narrative marker). سوی پوزش is a prepositional phrase 'toward (seeking) pardon'; نمایند is the 3rd‑person plural classical present/subjunctive meaning 'they make/do/show' (here 'they set out' or 'they go'); راه follows as the object/goal 'the way/road' — together 'they make way toward pardon.'
segments.s047-g37: چه is the interrogative 'what'; گفتند is the past 3rd‑person plural 'they said' (verb placed before the subject is common in poetic/classical word order); دانندگان خرد is the subject phrase 'the knowers of wisdom' = 'the wise.'
segments.s047-g38: که introduces the reported content ('that'). هر کس که ... is a standard correlative relative: 'whoever'/'every person who'. بد کرد is the past 'did evil' (بد = evil/bad; کرد = did). کیفر برد is an idiomatic clause 'bear/receive punishment' (کیفر = punishment). Note the double که: the first introduces the content clause, the second is the relative marker within 'هر کس که.'
segments.s047-g39: بماند is the 3rd‑person present/subjunctive form meaning 'let/it remains' or narratively 'remains'; به تیمار is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in/under care' (تیمار = care/treatment). و دل پر ز درد joins another predicative phrase 'and (their) heart full of pain' using پر ز = 'full of' + ز (poetic 'of/from') to mark source/cause.
segments.s047-g40: چو introduces a comparison or temporal clause 'as/just as'; ما مانده‌ایم is perfective/present perfect 'we have remained' (مانده‑ایم = we remain/are left). ای is the vocative particle 'O', followed by شه رادمرد 'king, man of magnanimity' (شه = king, رادمرد = noble/generous man).
segments.s047-g121: شنیدم is a past‑tense, first‑person singular verb 'I heard' and heads the clause; the following phrase همه هر چه گفتی سخن is its object (Persian permits verb before a long object). هر چه functions together as 'whatever'/'all that' modifying گفتی (here 'what you said'); سخن ('speech') is the noun being qualified.
segments.s047-g122: نگه کن is an imperative idiom meaning 'hold/stop/keep (it)' (نگه as noun/verb‑stem + کن imperative). که introduces the following clause with a causal/purpose sense ('for/so that'); پاسخ is the noun 'answer' and چه before the verb یابی asks 'what', while یابی is a present/subjunctive/optative form 'you may find / you will find' (verb at the end, typical SOV). ز بن is a prepositional phrase 'from the root/base'.
segments.s047-g123: بگو is the imperative 'say' (command to name or call). آن دو (textually present though 'دو' is not listed in the word array) selects 'those two' as the definite object; adjectives بی‌شرم and ناپاک follow the demonstrative in typical Persian order and qualify the two people; را is the definite/object marker tying this noun phrase to the verb بگو.
segments.s047-g124: This segment lists the names/labels to be applied: دو bیداد ('two injustices') uses دو as a numeral modifying بیداد; items are joined by و. بد modifies مهر as an epithet 'bad/evil affection' and ناباک is another epithet; the trailing را again marks the whole list as the (definite) object of بگو. Word order is a coordinated noun phrase enumerating labels to apply.
segments.s047-g125: که begins an explanatory/casual clause ('for'/'because'). گفتار is the subject 'speech' and خیره functions as a negative/derogatory modifier ('idle/foolish') qualifying گفتار. نیرزد is a stative verb 'is not worth' (third person; present tense used to state a general truth) and به چیز ('to/for a thing') completes the idiom 'نیرزد به چیز' = 'is worth nothing'.
segments.s047-g126: ازین در is a fixed phrase 'from this door/this matter/topic' (از + این + در), where ین is an orthographic variant of 'این' and در here has the literal 'door' image used for 'topic'. سخن خود is 'our/one's own speech' (خود is the possessive/emphatic). نرانیم is a first‑person‑plural jussive/subjunctive 'let us not drive away' or 'we should not drive away' (negative hortative sense), and نیز adds 'also/too', linking back to what was said.
segments.s047-g127: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). بر is the preposition 'upon/for' used here in the idiom بر + [person] + مهر خاست to mean 'love for [someone] arose'. منوچهرتان is the proper name Manuchehr with the second‑person plural possessive suffix -تان ('your Manuchehr' = the Manuchehr you revere or belong to). مهر is 'love/affection' and خاست is the past tense verb 'arose/began', so the clause reads 'if love for Manuchehr has arisen (in you)'.
segments.s047-g128: تن is the noun 'body' (here meaning the corpse/body of Iraj). ایرج is the proper name Iraj. نامورتان is نامور ('famous/renowned') with the possessive suffix -تان 'your' ('your famed one'). کجاست is the interrogative copula 'where is it?' (کجا 'where' + ست 'is'), so the whole phrase asks 'where is the body of Iraj, your famed one?'
segments.s047-g193: This is a pair of parallel prepositional phrases: 'به یک دست پیل' and 'به یک دست شیر' — the preposition به is used instrumentally/locative ('with / in'), followed by 'یک دست' ('one hand') and the noun carried. The conjunction و links the two balanced phrases; word order (به + numeral + noun + object) is a common poetic inversion for 'in one hand an elephant, in the other a lion.'
segments.s047-g194: 'جهان را' is the definite object marked by the enclitic را. The verb phrase is the compound اندر آوردن: 'به تخت اندر آورده' = 'has brought (into/on) the throne' (آورده is a past participle/perfect form used predicatively). The final زِیر here qualifies the result as subjection — 'under (the) [throne]' / 'subject to' — an archaic/poetic placement after the verb phrase.
segments.s047-g195: A compact noun phrase: ابر ("a cloud") followed by پشت پیلانش ('behind his elephants' — پشت = 'behind/rear' and پیلانش = 'his elephants' with the possessive). The appended prepositional phrase بر تخت زر ('upon the golden throne') places the scene; زر postmodifies تخت as an adjectival/attributive noun ('golden'). The sequence is poetic nominal description without an explicit verb.
segments.s047-g196: ز گوهر ('of/from gems' — ز is the poetic form of از), همه طوق ('a collar wholly/entirely') and شیران نر ('male lions') form a noun phrase describing ornament: 'a collar entirely of gems (worn by) the male lions.' The order (material first, then whole/collar, then possessor) is typical of elevated/poetic diction.
segments.s047-g197: تبیره زنان is an agent phrase: 'تبیره' appears to name an action or instrument associated with the women (contextually: musical accompaniment), but the provided lemma senses do not match that meaning (so the exact dictionary sense is unavailable). 'زنان پیش پیلان' = 'women before the elephants' (پیش = 'in the presence of'), and 'به پای' = 'at the feet (of)' — 'به' + 'پای' gives a locative 'at the feet'.
segments.s047-g198: ز هر سو = 'from every side' (ز = poetic از 'from', هر سو = 'each/every side'). خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'to roar'/'to sound forth' describing the action; it governs the following instrument or source 'کره نای' (the voice/sound of the reed‑pipe). The word کره here has no matching sense in the provided list for the intended meaning (so marked unknown), while نای is the reed‑flute (ney).
segments.s047-g199: تو گفتی که = 'you said that' (reported speech). The subordinate clause میدان بجوشد همی uses the present-subjunctive بجوشد to report an expected or predicted event ('would/shall boil' — here figurative 'the field would seethe'); همی is an archaic/enclitic particle adding continuous/ emphatic aspect. Note: the provided senses list for گفتی did not include the verbal sense 'you said', so that entry is marked -1.
segments.s047-g200: زمین (subject) + به آسمان (direction 'toward the sky') + بر خروشد همی (بر + خروشد) is a poetic verb phrase: خروشیدن 'to roar/come into tumult' with the prefix/particle بر giving an upward/intensive sense ('rise up/erupt'); همی again gives continuous/emphatic force. The clause is another reported prediction paralleling the previous one.
segments.s047-g129: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). کام is the noun 'desire' and is qualified by the nouns دد و دام ('hostility/hatred and trap'), joined by و. بودش is the past copula with the enclitic pronoun (literally 'it was his' / 'his was'), and نهفت (a past/participle form meaning 'hid/was hidden') closes the clause — i.e. his desire for malice and ambush he kept hidden.
segments.s047-g130: سرش را is the direct object 'his head' (سر + possessive -ش) marked by را. یکی is the numeral 'one'; تنگ is an attributive adjective 'narrow' modifying تابوت 'coffin'. جفت follows as a postmodifier meaning 'paired/double' (a poetic description of the coffin). Word order is typical classical Persian: determiner/adjective + noun, with جفت adding the image of a pair.
segments.s047-g131: کنون چون 'now that/when' introduces a temporal clause; ز before the name marks the target/source ('on/against Irāj' in poetic usage). بپرداختید is a past, 2nd-person plural verb meaning here 'you exacted/punished / you avenged' (literal 'you paid' in older usage).
segments.s047-g132: به کین is a prepositional phrase 'for/by way of vengeance' (به marking purpose). منوچهر names the object of that vengeance. بر + ساختید is a classical combination where بر (here 'against') plus the verb ساختید ('you made/raised') yields 'you set up/raised (force) against' — i.e. you made an attack against Manuchehr.
segments.s047-g133: نبینید is a negative imperative/optative (2nd plural) 'do not look/see'. رویش ('his face') is the object (روی + enclitic -ش); مگر 'except' introduces the condition limiting viewing: 'except with an army' (با سپاه: with an army). The clause thus forbids seeing him unless accompanied by troops.
segments.s047-g134: ز پولاد is a material/genitive phrase 'of/from steel' using poetic ز. بر سر نهاده is verb-final: بر here means 'upon', سر 'head', and نهاده (past participle/verb) 'placed/has placed' — 'a helmet placed upon the head'. کلاه ('hat' used as 'helmet') is the thing placed. Word order reflects classical Persian: material + verb phrase.
segments.s047-g135: ابا here is the line-initial form; in context it functions as با 'with' (a poetic/orthographic variant) to coordinate instruments: 'with a mace and with the Kayanian banner'. گرز is 'mace'; و is the coordinating conjunction; کاویانی qualifies درفش as 'Kayanian (i.e. Kayanid) standard/banner'.
segments.s047-g136: زمین کرده is a verb phrase where کرده ('has made/did') takes زمین ('the ground/earth') as its object — 'made the ground'. از سم اسپان بنفش is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'purple from the hooves of the horses': از 'from/of', سم 'hoof' (not 'poison' here), اسپان 'horses' (plural), بنفش 'purple' describing the result (the ground made purple).
segments.s047-g137: سپهدار is the noun meaning 'commander' (title) and is the topic of the clause. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by قارن as the point of comparison. رزم + زن is a noun + زن construction where رزم 'battle' modifies زن used verbally ('one who strikes') to form 'battle-striker' = 'warrior/fighter'. Word order is Topic — comparison — descriptive compound.
segments.s047-g138: چو again marks a comparison 'as/like'. شاپور is a proper name. و links the two comparisons. نستوه is an epithet modifying the following compound; here it functions adjectivally ('unyielding/distressed' in the lexicon but used as an epithet). شمشیر + زن is another noun+زن compound where شمشیر 'sword' + زن ('one who strikes') = 'swordsman'.
segments.s047-g139: به یک دست is an adverbial phrase 'with one hand' where به is instrumental/marking circumstance. شیدوش is a noun placed after 'hand' (here an attributive or appositive element — the exact denotation is archaic/poetic), and جنگی is an adjective 'warlike' qualifying that element. به پای follows as another locative phrase 'at the foot(s)/at the feet'. The sequence gives circumstantial details (how/where) rather than a full finite verb in this clause.
segments.s047-g140: چو introduces a comparison 'as/like'. شیروی is an adjective 'lion-like' modifying the proper name شیراوژن (Shirâwazhan). رهنمای is a noun 'guide' (agent) that apposits or titles that person. The phrase reads 'like the lion-like Shirâwazhan, the guide.'
segments.s047-g141: چو = 'like/as' introduces the comparison. سام نریمان are two names presented together (Sam, Nariman). و links them to سرو یمن, where سرو 'cypress' is an epithet meaning a tall/beautiful person and یمن specifies 'of Yemen' (epithet of origin). The phrase lists exemplars to which the subject is compared.
segments.s047-g142: به پیش سپاه is a locative phrase 'before/in front of the army' with به + پیش marking position. اندرون adds 'within/inside' emphasising placement in the host. رای زن is a compound where رای 'counsel/plan' plus زن ('one who does/strikes') yields 'counsel-giver' = 'counsellor'. Together: 'a counsellor within the front of the host.'
segments.s047-g143: درختی is an indefinite noun 'a tree'. که is the relative pronoun 'that/which' introducing the relative clause. از + کین 'from/of vengeance' gives the source ('from the vengeance'), ایرج is the proper name Iraj, and برست is an archaic past-form meaning roughly 'sprang forth/was born/burst'. Grammatically this is a noun + relative clause describing the tree's origin.
segments.s047-g144: به خون is an instrumental phrase 'with blood' or 'by blood'. برگ و بارش combines برگ 'leaf' and بار + -ش (possessive) meaning 'its fruit/load' so 'its leaves and fruit'. بخواهیم شست is a periphrastic future: خواهیم (we will/wish) + past participle شست (washed) yields 'we will wash'. The whole clause: 'with blood we will wash its leaves and fruit.'
segments.s047-g113: Two coordinated simple past clauses joined by و: فرستاده گفت (the messenger said) and سپهبد شنید (the spahbed heard). Both verbs are perfect/past (گفت, شنید) and the subjects precede their verbs; the second clause has its object implied and is completed by the next segment.
segments.s047-g114: مر is an archaic/poetic dative 'to me'. آن بند را is a marked definite direct object 'that stanza' (را marks definiteness). پاسخ آمد is an impersonal/experiential construction 'an answer came' and کلید follows as the noun/predicate 'the key' — the line therefore means 'to me came the key, the answer to that stanza.' Word order is poetic/inverted: dative + object before the verb, predicate after the verb.
segments.s047-g115: چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause; بشنید is past 3rd singular 'he heard'. شاه جهان is a title 'king of the world' (compound noun), and کدخدای here names the source/agent of the message (possessor of the following پیام). The order places the hearer (شاه جهان) before the possessed noun phrase that follows.
segments.s047-g116: پیام is the head 'message/tidings'; دو فرزند is the possessor 'of two sons' and ناپاک رای is a compound modifier 'of impure counsel/plan' (ناپاک modifies رای). Together this yields 'the message of two sons of impure counsel' — رای here means 'counsel/plan', not 'vote'.
segments.s047-g117: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' / 'each', modifying the optative clause بمرد ('may he die' — a jussive/optative form expressing a wish or curse). گرانمایه is a vocative or appositive 'the esteemed/noble one' and گفت is the reporting verb 'said'. Sequence is speech-content first (the curse) followed by the reporter, a common narrative order.
segments.s047-g118: که introduces reported speech or content ('that'/'saying that'). خورشید را is the definite object 'the sun' (را marks definiteness). چون is an interrogative 'how' here, توانی is 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you can/are able' and نهفت is the verb 'hide/conceal' (archaic/poetic form). Together they form an indirect question: 'how could you hide the sun?'.
segments.s047-g119: نهان دل is a nominal compound 'hidden heart(s)' (i.e., secret intent). آن دو مرد پلید is the possessor phrase 'those two vile men' (آن + numeral + noun + adjective). The genitive relationship is expressed by placing the possessor after the possessed noun, a poetic but regular pattern.
segments.s047-g120: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/than' here used in a comparative sense. خورشید is 'the sun' and روشن‌تر is the comparative adjective 'brighter / more clear'. آمد پدید is a verbal phrase 'came forth / appeared' (آمد + پدید = 'became manifest'). Together: '... appeared, more clear than the sun.'
segments.s047-g161: بیامد is a simple past verb 'came'. به کردار is an adverbial simile ('به' + noun) meaning 'in the manner of / like', so 'به کردار باد دمان' functions as one unit: a noun phrase used adverbially, literally 'in the fashion of the wind of the hem' (i.e. like a breeze around the skirts). Word order keeps the simile after the verb: verb + manner phrase.
segments.s047-g162: This is two balanced noun phrases joined without an explicit conjunction: 'سری پر ز پاسخ' = 'a head full (پر) of answers (پاسخ)' (ز here functions as the poetic/genitive marker 'of/from'), and 'دلی پرگمان' = 'a heart full of suspicion' (پرگمان is a compound 'پر + گمان' = 'full of doubt'). دلی is a noun/adjectival form relating to 'دل' (heart), and the adjective 'پرگمان' follows it.
segments.s047-g163: ز دیدار here sets the scene ('from/at the sight' or 'on seeing / at the meeting'). چون introduces a temporal clause ('when/when suddenly'), خاور is the subject 'the easterner' (or 'the one from the east') and آمد پدید is a compound verbal expression: آمد (came) + پدید (appeared) — together 'came into view / appeared'.
segments.s047-g164: به هامون کشیده is a participial/ adjectival phrase ('drawn/stretched toward the plain') modifying سراپرده ('the inner curtain / inner chamber behind the veil'). The verb دید is the main verb of the clause ('he saw'). Word order: locative/qualifying phrase + noun + verb ('he saw the inner curtain stretched toward the plain').
segments.s047-g165: بیامد is past 'came'. به درگاه پرده سرای is a locative/goal phrase: به (to) + درگاه (threshold/court) + پرده سرای (a genitive compound 'parde-sarā' = 'the chamber/house of the curtain' i.e. the curtained inner court). The whole phrase means 'he came to the gate/threshold of the curtain-chamber.'
segments.s047-g166: به پرده درون is a locative phrase 'inside the curtain' (به … درون = 'within the curtain'). بود is the past copula 'was'. The final pair 'خاور خدای' is a nominal phrase: خاور (the Easterner / the one from the east) followed by خدای, here functioning as an epithet or title rather than the vocative exclamation; together they name or describe who was inside. Word order is locative → copula → subject phrase.
segments.s047-g167: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (introducing one item). خیمه پرنیان is a noun phrase 'a tent (خیمه) of silk/silken (پرنیان)' with the adjective after the noun — typical Persian order. ساخته is a past participle/past form 'made/constructed' completing the predicate: 'a silken tent had been made.'
segments.s047-g168: ستاره زده is a compound participial adjective 'star-struck / star-marked' (i.e. decorated with star-patterns). جای پرداخته: جای 'place' and پرداخته (past participle of پرداختن) 'worked on / elaborated / adorned'. Together they describe the setting of the tent: 'a place ornamented with star-designs.' Word order puts the descriptive phrase after the first clause.
segments.s047-g241: چنان ... چون بود is a correlative comparative: چنان 'so/such' sets the degree, followed by adjectives 'نامور' (renowned) and the negative compound 'بی‌هنر' (without skill). چون here means 'as', and بود is the past copula 'was' that closes the comparative clause.
segments.s047-g242: کش functions as an agentive/compound element attached to the following noun (a poetic, compact epithet), with 'آموزگار' the head 'teacher' and 'آفریدون' the name in apposition/possession; the final بود is the past copula. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (epithet + noun + possessor + copula).
segments.s047-g243: نبیره is the subject 'the scion/young descendant'. چو شد is a temporal clause marker 'when it became/when there came to be', and رای here means 'plan/counsel'. زن با نیا links two sources of counsel ('the woman's with the ancestor's') with با 'with'. The clause says that the scion's decision aligned with the woman and the ancestor.
