segments.s047-g244: ازان is the archaic fused form of از آن 'from that'; جایگه is a poetic variant of جایگاه 'place'. بردمد is a past tense verb 'carried off / bore away'. کیمیا is the object carried off (here treated as the thing/person taken). The phrase is straightforward: they bore Kimiyā away from that place.
segments.s047-g245: بباید is an impersonal modal 'it is necessary/one must'. بسیچید is here an action 'muster/gather' presented as the required verb (third-person plural sense implied). ما را is the definite object 'us' (را marks definiteness), and بجنگ 'for war/into battle' indicates purpose. Together: 'It is necessary that they muster us for war.'
segments.s047-g246: This is an infinitival purposive phrase: شتاب آوریدن literally 'to bring haste' = 'to hasten'. به جای is the prepositional phrase 'in place of / instead of', and درنگ is the noun 'delay/tarrying'. So the clause expresses the need 'to hasten instead of delaying.'
segments.s047-g247: ز is the poetic form of از 'from'. لشکر سواران is a genitive phrase 'the host/army of horsemen' (lshkr = army, سواران = horsemen). برون 'out/forth' is an adverb, and تاختند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they rushed/charged/attacked'. The clause describes the horsemen rushing forth.
segments.s047-g248: Another poetic listing with ز = 'from': ز چین و ز خاور 'from Chin (China) and from the East'; سپه is 'army/host' as the object, and ساختند is the past 'they made / they formed'. The clause means they raised/formed an army drawn from those regions.
segments.s047-g81: به + adjective + noun here functions as a prepositional phrase meaning 'with' or 'by means of'; زرین is an adjective 'golden' modifying the following nouns عمود ('pillar/column') and کمر ('girdle/belt'). The two phrases are linked by و ('and'), yielding 'with golden pillars and with a golden girdle.'
segments.s047-g82: زمین کرده is a verbal/predicative unit: زمین (ground) + کرده (made/caused) yields 'the ground was made' or 'had been made'; خورشیدگون is an adjectival compound 'sun-colored/sun-like' describing that ground; سر به سر is an idiom meaning 'throughout' or 'entirely' (literally 'head to head'), so the whole clause means 'the ground was made sun-colored all over.' Note the participial/past reference of کرده marking a completed action.
segments.s047-g83: دو رویه here means 'two rows/lines' (a poetic use where رویه functions as a line/face of people) and بزرگان is the genitive/attributive 'of the great ones' (the nobles). کشیده is an adjective 'stretched/outstretched' or 'drawn out', and رده ('rank/row') reinforces the idea of ordered formation: 'two rows of the great ones drawn out in rank.'
segments.s047-g84: سراپای یکسر is a fixed intensifying phrase: سراپا 'from head to foot' plus یکسر 'entirely/utterly' — 'entirely head to toe.' به زر means 'in gold' (به marking the material/covering), and آژده functions as an adjective meaning 'alarming/terrifying' — together: 'entirely, head to toe in gold, terrifying.'
segments.s047-g85: به یک دست here means 'in/at one hand' (به marking the location), بربسته is a participial adjective 'bound/restrained' modifying شیر و پلنگ 'lion and leopard.' Word order places the location phrase first, then the described objects: 'with one hand (there were) a bound lion and leopard.'
segments.s047-g86: به دست دگر = 'in the other hand' (دگر = 'other'). ژنده is an adjective/noun 'ragged' or 'rag' modifying پیلان (plural of پیل 'elephant'), and جنگ here qualifies پیلان as 'of war' ('war‑elephants' or 'elephants for war'). The phrase therefore reads 'in the other hand, the ragged (things) of the war‑elephants' — the text compresses a compound image (likely tattered war‑equipment or trophies associated with elephants).
segments.s047-g87: برون شد is a simple past compound meaning 'went out' or 'emerged' (برون = 'out', شد = past auxiliary/verb). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and درگاه شاپور = 'Shapur's court/presence.' گرد here appears as an adverbial/poetic particle meaning 'about/around' attached to motion; overall: 'went out from Shapur's court.'
segments.s047-g88: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger.' سلم را is a definite object phrase (سلم here is a proper name in context — the list did not include a name sense), پیش برد is a verb phrase 'brought forward' or 'led before (the king)' (پیش = 'before/in presence of', برد here = 'took/brought'). Grammatically, the messenger is performing the action of bringing Salm forward.
segments.s047-g249: فتاد is a past, impersonal/perfective verb here meaning 'befell' or 'arose' (an event happened). اندران is a locative phrase 'among them' modifying فتاد (i.e. 'it fell/occurred among them'). بوم و بر is a fixed paired phrase 'land and domain/realm' (بوم = homeland, بر = region/territory); the sequence places the topical noun phrase before the rest of the predicate in typical poetic inversion. گفت‌گوی immediately follows as the noun 'discussion/dialogue' that the verb brings about.
segments.s047-g250: جهانی here qualifies گفت‌گوی (گفت‌گوی جهانی = 'a worldly/realm-related discussion'). بدیشان is an indirect-object/locative expression 'to/for them' (pointing back to those among whom the talk arose). نهادند is the past transitive 'they placed/set' used idiomatically with روی (نهادنِ روی) to mean 'to set one's face/turn one's attention to.' روی is the noun 'face' used idiomatically: 'they set/placed their faces upon' = 'they turned their faces/attention to it.'
segments.s047-g251: سپاهی is a collective noun 'army/host' here. که introduces a relative clause ('which/that'). آن را uses the demonstrative plus object-marking را ('that one' as the thing being spoken of). کرانه نبود is a negated past copula: کرانه 'boundary/edge' + نبود 'was not' meaning 'had no boundary' or 'was without limit.' Word order is standard: head noun + relative clause.
segments.s047-g252: بدان بد is an idiomatic/poetic phrase meaning 'to that bad degree' → 'so bad/so dire'; here the preposition/prefix ب (ب-) combines with the demonstrative/degree-form to form 'to that (degree)' and is followed by بد 'bad/ill'. که introduces the result clause. اختر جوانه نبود is a verbless/result clause with نبود 'was not' meaning 'there was no budding star' (اختر = star; جوانه = 'bud'/'incipient' → 'a young/incipient/star' = a young/star about to rise).
segments.s047-g253: ز خاور is a prepositional phrase 'from the east' (ز = 'from' in poetic style). دو لشکر is the subject 'two armies/hosts.' به ایران is the directional phrase 'to/into Iran.' کشید is a past tense verb meaning 'drew/marched/advanced' (کشیدن used here in the sense 'to move/withdraw/advance toward'); its poetic use often denotes movement of forces toward a place.
segments.s047-g254: بخفتان is an archaic/poetic verbal/adverbial form meaning roughly 'they lay low / lay in ambush / slept (i.e. hid)' — here it describes the manner of their action after advancing. و خود is coordinate 'and they themselves' (خود emphatic). اندرون 'inside/within' is the locative complement and ناپدید is an adjective/predicative participle 'vanished/unseen' (they disappeared into the interior). The clause therefore states consequent action: they lay low/kept hidden and then themselves vanished into the inner quarters.
segments.s047-g255: ابا is a problematic/archaic form in this line (the lemma list gives meanings related to refusal); here it functions as a prepositional/participial marker parallel to the following با — rendered in sense as 'with' or 'wearing' in poetic usage. ژنده is an adjective 'rag/ragged' and پیلان (plural of پیل) 'elephants' — the phrase evokes 'ragged elephant-gear' or 'in rags like elephants' (a poetic image). و با خواسته coordinates another instrumental phrase: با 'with' + خواسته 'desire/intent' (i.e. 'with intent/with a purpose').
segments.s047-g256: دو خونی is a compound adjective 'two-blooded' or 'blood-stained / bloodthirsty' (دو used distributively/emphatically with خونی 'bloody'). به introduces the result/target 'to' and کینه دل آراسته is a verbal/adjectival phrase: کینه 'hatred' + دل 'heart' (possessive relationship: hatred in the heart) + آراسته 'adorned' (past participle used predicatively) — literally 'they adorned the heart with hatred' = 'they filled/adorned their hearts with hatred.'
segments.s047-g233: دو مرد جفا‌پیشه را is a fronted, definite noun phrase marked by را (emphasizing/defining “the two cruel men”); دل ز درد follows and literally names the affected thing (“the heart, from pain”). The preposition-like ز introduces the cause (“from/because of pain”), so the whole segment prepares the subject/affected noun (دل) that the verb in the next segment will act on.
segments.s047-g234: بپیچید is the finite verb meaning ‘twisted/writhed’ (classical b- prefix with past sense), agreeing with دل as the affected thing; و شد links resultatively to the following clause. رویشان is رو + possessive شان (‘their face/complexion’), and لاژورد is a predicate adjective/noun giving the resulting color (‘lapis/azure’).
segments.s047-g235: نشستند و جستند combines two past actions: نشستند (‘they sat’) and جستند here means ‘they sought/searched’ (contextual use). هرگونه رای is an object phrase ‘every kind of counsel/plan’ explaining what they were seeking; the verb follows the subject in typical Persian order.
segments.s047-g236: سخن را places ‘the speech/talk’ as a definite topic (را). نه ... نه is a correlative negative (‘neither ... nor’); سر and پای are used idiomatically as ‘beginning/head’ and ‘end/foot’ respectively, and بود پیدا (‘was apparent/visible’) states that the speech had neither start nor finish.
segments.s047-g237: به marks the indirect object/direction (‘to’); سلم بزرگ is a noun phrase ‘the great Salm’ (here a proper name) and آنگهی is a temporal adverb ‘then/thereupon’. تور is the speaker (proper name) and گفت is the verb ‘said’ introducing the following content clause.
segments.s047-g238: که introduces a reported-content clause (‘that’). آرام و شادی are the coordinated abstract nouns ‘calm and joy’; بباید is an impersonal modal ‘ought/should/must’, and نهفت (here) functions as the verb/infinitive ‘be hidden’ or ‘be kept concealed’ — together meaning they should be kept hidden.
segments.s047-g239: نباید که is an impersonal negative modal construction meaning ‘it must not be that/one should not’. آن بچه نره‌شیر is the noun phrase ‘that cub of the male-lion’ (آن for ‘that’, بچه as young/offspring, نره‌شیر specifying it is the male-lion’s cub). The clause is introduced as something forbidden or undesirable.
segments.s047-g240: شود and گردد are two verbs in sequence meaning ‘become’ (شود) and ‘turn/become’ (گردد); they present the undesired developments: تیزدندان (‘sharp-toothed’, adjectival) and دلیر (‘brave’). The coordinating و links the two predicted states.
segments.s047-g225: چنو is an adverbial opening ('چنان/چنو' = 'thus/when so'). بست is the past verb (here in the idiom کوس بستن) meaning ‘beat/strike (the drum)’ — a poetic usage; بر governs the locative phrase 'on/upon'. کوهه (mountain) is the locative noun governed by بر, and پیل کوس is a compound describing the drum as elephant-sized or mighty (پیل qualifies کوس in a compressed, poetic order). Together the segment says 'when thus the elephant-drum was beaten on the mountain.'
segments.s047-g226: هوا is the subject 'the air/atmosphere'. گردد is the present/subjunctive form of 'گشتن' used as 'become/turn'. از گرد gives the cause/source ('from the dust') where گرد here means 'dust'. چون آبنوس is a simile 'like ebony' (predicative), so the whole clause means 'the air became, from the dust, like ebony.'
segments.s047-g227: گر sets a conditional 'if'. آیند is the present/subjunctive/plural 'they come' used for a hypothetical future. زی ما is an archaic/prepositional phrase 'toward/against us' (classical usage; modern Persian would use به or علیه), ما = 'us'. به جنگ 'to battle' marks purpose/encounter, and آن گروه 'that band/troop' is the subject of آیند. The phrase means 'If that troop come against us to fight...'.
segments.s047-g228: شود is the prospective/attributive 'become'/'will be'. The line uses a chiasmic parallel: 'کوه هامون و هامون کوه' — the nouns are swapped to create the image that mountains become plains and plains become mountains; the inversion is emphatic and poetic rather than literal syntactic complication.
segments.s047-g229: همه دل is the subject 'all hearts' (everyone’s heart). پر از + noun means 'full of ...' so پر از کین و پرچین = 'full of hatred and (something like) rancor/anger' (پرچین here is a poetic/archaic item). بروی is a verbal form from رفتن ('they went/they set forth') used here to describe their movement or disposition collectively — poetically compressed after the noun phrase. Overall the segment says their hearts were filled with hostile feeling and they set out/turned (for battle).
segments.s047-g230: به جز = 'except'; جنگشان is 'their battle/war' (جنگ + possessive suffix). نیست negates existence ('there is not'). چیز آرزوی is a nominal phrase 'a thing of desire' (object of نیست). Word order here ('به جز X نیست چیز آرزوی') yields the sense 'there is nothing they desire except X' — i.e. 'they desire nothing except the battle.'
segments.s047-g231: بریشان is the prepositional dative 'for/to them' (بر + ایشان fused). همه 'all' is the direct object scope, and برشمرد is the past tense verb 'enumerated/recounted'. آنچه is the relative/pronoun 'that which/what' and دید is past 'saw' — 'he recounted to them everything that he had seen.' The order (object and dative before verb) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s047-g232: سخن نیز = 'the speech/word/ tale also' (نیز = 'also'). کز is the classical relative/complementary particle meaning 'which/from which' and here indicates source: 'that (he) heard from Afrīdun'. شنید is past 'heard'. Together: 'and also the words (that) he heard from Afrīdun.'

sentences.s047-s73: From that time until now, no one sought his vengeance whom we saw to be truly backed by Fortune.
sentences.s047-s74: You came ill with your two sons; where was the fighting begun by taking the first hand?
sentences.s047-s75: Now from that tree which the enemy struck, a vigorous branch arose, tall.
sentences.s047-s76: May he now come like Hejbar Zhiyān, his breast tightly bound with his father's vengeance.
sentences.s047-s33: Let us go so that we may give water and hardship; when the crown is renewed, we will give crown and treasure.
sentences.s047-s34: A messenger came, his heart full of words; the speech had neither head nor root to be seen.
sentences.s047-s35: With an elephant, with treasure, and with a request, he came adorned to the king's court.
sentences.s047-s36: The news reached King Afrīdūn; he commanded that the imperial throne (be prepared).
sentences.s047-s37: They dressed him in Chinese brocade and decked him with the Kayanian crown.
sentences.s047-s38: He sat upon the king's turquoise throne; like a dark cypress, the moon's circle was about his head.
sentences.s047-s39: With a robe, with the crown, and with a necklace and earrings — thus he was fitting for a sovereign.
sentences.s047-s40: The auspicious Manuchehr, on the king's hand, sat down and placed (it) upon his head over the hat.
sentences.s047-s5: So they sent someone to Fereydun to beg pardon; where else was there recourse? This was enough.
sentences.s047-s6: From that assembly they chose two; one (of them) was a pure‑hearted, eloquent man.
sentences.s047-s7: They addressed that man — intelligent, of counsel and modest — and spoke with very urgent entreaty.
sentences.s047-s8: They opened again the eastern treasure‑house; they saw a fearful descent from on high.
sentences.s047-s9: From the treasury they sought jewels and a golden crown, and they adorned the elephants' backs.
sentences.s047-s10: Upon the chariots they placed—what musk and perfume, what brocade, dinars, fur, and silk.
sentences.s047-s11: With the elephant — the overturner/upturner of the heavens — and with colour and scent, they set their faces from the East toward Iran.
sentences.s047-s12: Everyone who came poorly to the king's gate, he sent them away one by one as a keepsake.
sentences.s047-s85: The two kings, rulers of two realms, sat together in private and said, "The messenger has returned."
sentences.s047-s86: Then the commander arrived as well, bearing the burden, and brought the messenger before the king.
sentences.s047-s87: They set up a new assembly-place and asked the new king for news of the ritual/ceremony.
sentences.s047-s88: They sought information of every kind, from the villagers and from the imperial throne.
sentences.s047-s13: When their hearts were made ready from desire, the messenger came, well‑adorned.
sentences.s047-s14: They delivered a message to Fereydun; first they carried the name of the sovereign.
sentences.s047-s15: May Fereydun be eternal everywhere; may Providence grant him all nobility.
sentences.s047-s16: May his head be green and his body esteemed; may his character rise above the high heavens.
sentences.s047-s77: The messenger beheld that fearful utterance; he saw Manuchehr the lord seated.
sentences.s047-s78: He withered and rose, trembling from his place; then he put his foot into the saddle.
sentences.s047-s79: That noble young man, of bright soul, beheld all things that exist.
sentences.s047-s80: For, with Salm and Tur under the revolving heavens, wrinkles will not long be brought upon his face.
sentences.s047-s21: Thus it had been written to us by Būsh; a practice in Būsh's manner came into being.
sentences.s047-s22: Hezbar the world‑consuming and the male dragon also cannot be freed from the snare of fate.
sentences.s047-s23: And another: the impure demon's command carried away the heart from fear of the world's khedive.
sentences.s047-s24: Such an evil counsel befell us and stunned us, so that the minds of the two sons became an evil seat.
sentences.s047-s45: When the messenger saw the king's court, he came running on foot from the road.
sentences.s047-s46: When he came near King Afrīdūn, he saw his head, throne, and lofty crown.
